explicit_text: I have a unique role in the United States Park Police aviation unit as one of only two members who are neither pilots nor paramedics. I serve as the assistant commander and fill in as the commander when needed, handling administrative duties like staffing assignments, budgeting, and procurement. On September 11, 2001, we responded quickly to the Pentagon incident, coordinating helicopter operations and securing landing zones. My experience with the Park Police has taught me the importance of training and quick decision-making in emergency situations.

explicit_text: On the morning of September 11, 2001, I first heard about the attacks while checking fuel trucks near our hangar. We saw the planes hit the World Trade Center live on TV and quickly realized it was a coordinated attack. Shortly after, we got reports of a plane crashing into the Pentagon, and our unit launched helicopters to assist with medical evacuations. The day was intense, with multiple casualties and concerns about further attacks, but everyone stayed focused and professional throughout the response.

explicit_text: Within 20-30 minutes of the World Trade Center attacks on September 11, 2001, we heard reports of a plane crashing near the Pentagon. Our unit quickly prepared and launched helicopters, including Eagle One and Eagle Two, to assist with medical evacuations. We landed about 150-200 yards from the impact site on a roadway, possibly U.S. Route 110, and secured the landing zone while medical personnel attended to casualties. The scene was smoky and chaotic but orderly, with many people evacuating the building calmly despite the destruction.

explicit_text: During the Pentagon response on September 11, 2001, I heard over the police radio that the Reagan tower was evacuating due to smoke from the incident drifting toward the airport. The control of the airport's airspace was temporarily handed over to Eagle One, which was airborne at the time. Shortly after, all air traffic was diverted from the Washington area, easing the burden on Eagle One. Our direct communication line with the Reagan tower was crucial for coordinating the emergency response.

explicit_text: Eagle One and Eagle Two, the helicopters we used on September 11, 2001, are both Bell 412 models. These helicopters are large and sit on skids, with rotor blades high enough to avoid contact with people, though the tail rotor remains a hazard. On that day, Eagle Two was outfitted with a mass casualty kit to transport up to four injured patients instead of the usual two. The Bell 412s were vital in quickly evacuating injured personnel from the Pentagon crash site to medical facilities.

explicit_text: Eagle One, a Bell 412 helicopter, was the first to launch from our unit on September 11, 2001, to respond to the Pentagon crash. The crew included Ron Galey, John Marsh, and John Dillon, and they stayed airborne to control the airspace near the Pentagon while we prepared Eagle Two. Eagle One also took over air traffic control responsibilities from the Reagan tower when it was evacuated due to smoke. Later, Eagle One landed to transport patients but had to reposition temporarily due to reports of an unidentified aircraft approaching the Pentagon.

explicit_text: On September 11, 2001, Eagle Two, another Bell 412 helicopter, was prepared with a mass casualty kit to carry up to four injured patients. Our crew included Sergeant Keith Bohn as pilot, Sergeant Ken Burchell, Lieutenant Philip Cholak, and medical personnel from USUHS. We landed near the Pentagon crash site to secure the landing zone while others attended to casualties. Eagle Two transported two patients to MEDSTAR at the Washington Hospital Center, playing a critical role in the emergency medical response.

Having USUHS personnel on board enhanced our ability to handle the mass casualty situation effectively.

Although I'm less familiar with Virginia's roads, I recall that the proximity to the Pentagon made it a critical location for our emergency response. The state's location was central to the events that unfolded that day.
